L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states Brett was on his last day in Lauterbrunnen and 'desperate to get one final jump in before his flight home the next morning.' This last-chance urgency was a clearly identifiable pressure, separate from the merits of the wind assessment, that drove him to proceed despite companions warning him and walking down.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform gear check
L2_preconditions.description: Neither the jumper nor his companion performed a pre-jump gear check. The companion explicitly states that running a hand along the bridle routing would have immediately caught the misrouted bridle. This omission allowed the rigging error made during packing to persist undetected to the exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: Complete flat light conditions — heavy fresh snow, no shadows, no contrast, no texture on the snowfield — created a visual illusion that eliminated all depth cues. The helmet cam confirms he perceived himself to be much higher than he was, maintaining a relaxed posture with zero corrective input until impact. This visual illusion directly caused the fatal altitude misperception that the decision to fly in flat light enabled.

L2_preconditions.label: Flat Light Eliminating Depth Cues
L2_preconditions.description: Heavy fresh snow with no shadows, contrast, or texture on the snowfield created complete flat light that physically eliminated the visual depth cues required for terrain proximity assessment. This environmental condition directly degraded the jumper's ability to perceive altitude and terrain proximity during flight, making the consequences of the decision to fly proximity in these conditions unsurvivable.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was heavily intoxicated the night before and still visibly impaired at the time of the jump — splitting headache, vomiting twice on the hike, visibly unsteady. This acute hangover state directly degraded his physical coordination, contributing to his legs buckling on the push-off and the resulting unstable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper fixated entirely on recovering stability during freefall to the exclusion of the higher-priority task of deploying the parachute. This channelized attention on regaining a belly-to-earth position prevented him from executing the fundamental priority of pulling, despite approximately 6 seconds of available freefall time.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper had jumped hung over before without issues and that the decision to jump impaired was not unusual for him. This indicates a sustained pattern of jumping while substance-impaired — a normalization of deviance that enabled the conditions present on this fatal jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Tracking Suit Training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 2 skydives in the tracking suit and zero cliff jumps with it. Accepted practice requires 40-50 tracking suit skydives before BASE use, with mastery of ideal body position for maximum forward speed and glide. This profound training deficit meant the jumper lacked the physical skill to fly the suit effectively, directly enabling the fatal outcome when he chose to jump it at this site.

L2_preconditions.label: Physical Overexertion / Fatigue
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper drove 14 hours straight through the night and was visibly exhausted at the exit point. This acute fatigue from sleep deprivation likely degraded his judgment capacity, contributing to his decision to proceed with the unfamiliar suit at a demanding site despite a more experienced jumper's suggestion to start with something more forgiving.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence from Video Observation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper dismissed a suggestion to start with a more forgiving exit, stating the site 'looked easy from the footage.' This indicates the jumper unreasonably overestimated his capability based on watching videos rather than actual experience, substituting vicarious observation for the progression experience he lacked with the tracking suit.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper stated he had done this exit dozens of times and 'knew the heading by feel,' asserting he could navigate by feel alone despite zero visibility. This unreasonable overestimation of his ability to safely track past terrain features he could not see — when every other jumper present recognized the conditions as unjumpable and packed up to hike down — directly drove the decision to exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Zero visibility from fog
L2_preconditions.description: Low-level clouds reduced visibility to essentially zero, obscuring the fjord, landing area, and wall below the exit. This environmental condition directly prevented the jumper from seeing the protruding ledge he tracked into, making terrain avoidance during freefall physically impossible regardless of skill or familiarity with the site.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: Marcus became fixated on adjusting his helmet-mounted GoPro during flight, channeling his attention onto the camera to the exclusion of maintaining tracking body position, monitoring terrain proximity, and initiating deployment. This attentional fixation on a secondary task directly enabled the sustained loss of situational awareness described in the L1 error.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper experienced at least four near-miss stalls over three months due to poor angle of attack control, explicitly acknowledged the problem to peers, identified the specific corrective action (50 skydiving stall recovery training jumps), yet never followed through. This sustained pattern of inaction over multiple weekends directly enabled the continued decision to fly proximity terrain with the uncorrected deficiency.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: Multiple witnesses stated he 'completely froze the moment he saw the wires directly in front of him' — his arms stayed at his sides with no toggle or riser input despite approximately two seconds available. This is consistent with an automatic startle/freeze response triggered by the unexpected sight of guy wires directly ahead, rendering him temporarily unable to initiate any corrective action.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Antenna Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only two previous antenna jumps, both from taller structures where guy wires were further from the exit point. This antenna presented guy wires approximately 40 feet from the flight path — a significantly more constrained environment than his prior antenna experience. His insufficient antenna-specific experience likely contributed to his inability to execute an immediate heading correction under the time pressure and proximity demands of this object.

L2_preconditions.label: Failed to replace damaged PC
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's pilot chute had a ~4-inch tear in the mesh from the previous jump. Despite being advised by companions to replace it and being offered spare PCs, the jumper failed to swap the damaged component before jumping. Replacing a known-damaged PC is a basic equipment preparation action that would have directly prevented the total malfunction.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate multi-way planning
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states that nobody properly went through the exit order or separation plan, no specific delays were set, and no one discussed flight paths or opening altitudes. This failure to coordinate staged openings and horizontal/vertical separation directly created the conditions in which David tracked over another jumper and deployed into that jumper's airspace without awareness of the conflict. Accepted practice for multi-way jumps requires pre-planned separation, staged openings, and briefed flight paths.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to correct known deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was aware of his progressively longer delays through repeated warnings from other jumpers over the past season. Despite this feedback, he failed to correct the pattern, continuing to extend delays from 4 to 6 to 8+ seconds. This sustained normalization of deviance directly enabled the fatally excessive delay on this jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Experience with Expert Wingsuit
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had recently upgraded to an expert-level wingsuit (Aura 1) with only approximately 10 skydives in it before using it for BASE. Accepted progression norms require 40-60 wingsuit skydives with the specific suit before BASE use. The narrative explicitly states that expert-level wingsuits have stall boundaries requiring many flights to understand, and he had not put in that time. This insufficient familiarity with the suit's flight envelope directly contributed to his failure to recognize and recover from the developing stall.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient Skydiving Experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 5 skydives total, far below the accepted minimum of 200+ skydives before beginning BASE. This complete lack of foundational skydiving training meant the jumper had no basis for evaluating the decision to attempt a slider-up terminal cliff jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Lack of BASE Specific Training
L2_preconditions.description: With zero BASE jumps and only 5 skydives, the jumper had no BASE training, no sub-terminal tracking ability, no experience with BASE exits, and no object avoidance skills. This absence of any relevant training directly produced the unstable exit and failure to achieve adequate separation from the cliff.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper attempted a terminal slider-up cliff jump in a tracking suit with only 5 skydives and zero BASE jumps, a decision so far beyond any reasonable self-assessment that it demonstrates an extreme overestimation of personal capability. No reasonably calibrated individual would attempt this jump at this experience level.
